questions.text: You accidentally committed a file with your API key. How do you remove it from the last commit?
questions.context: A commit is like saving your code at a point in time. An API key is a secret password for external services.

options.text: Delete the file and push again
Use git reset to undo the commit, remove the file, recommit
Delete the entire repository and start over

questions.explanation: git reset --soft HEAD~1 undoes the last commit while keeping your changes. You can then remove the file and recommit safely.

questions.text: Your app shows a blank white screen. What's the FIRST thing you should check?
questions.context: When an app breaks, there are clues that tell you what went wrong — if you know where to look.

options.text: Open the browser console to check for error messages

questions.explanation: The browser console (F12) shows error messages that tell you exactly what went wrong and where. Always check errors before making changes.

questions.text: Which of these is the MOST dangerous security mistake in a web app?
questions.context: Security means protecting your app and users from people trying to do bad things with it.

options.text: Storing API keys in your frontend JavaScript code

questions.explanation: API keys in frontend code are visible to anyone who views your page source. Attackers can steal them and run up charges on your accounts.

questions.text: When a user clicks 'Submit' on a login form, what happens?
questions.context: Frontend is what users see in their browser. Backend is the server that processes requests.

options.text: The password is checked right in the browser
The browser sends a request to the backend, which checks the password against the database
The form emails the password to the developer

questions.explanation: Login credentials must be verified on the server (backend), not in the browser. The browser sends an HTTP request, the server checks the database, and responds with success or failure.

questions.text: You have a 'users' table and a 'posts' table. How do you connect them so each post belongs to a user?
questions.context: A database table is like a spreadsheet. A foreign key is a column that points to a row in another table.

options.text: Store the user's name in each post row
Add a user_id column to the posts table that references the users table
Put all posts inside the users table

questions.explanation: A foreign key (user_id in posts) creates a relationship between tables. This is the standard way to connect related data in a relational database.

questions.text: Your app works perfectly on your computer but crashes when deployed. What's the most likely cause?
questions.context: Deploying means putting your app on the internet so others can use it. Environment variables are settings that differ between your computer and the server.

options.text: Missing environment variables on the server
The server doesn't have a monitor
Your code only works on Macs

questions.explanation: Environment variables (database URLs, API keys, etc.) exist on your local machine but need to be configured separately on the server. This is the #1 cause of 'works on my machine' problems.

questions.text: Why do developers write tests for their code?
questions.context: Tests are code that automatically checks if your other code works correctly.

options.text: To make the code run faster
To catch bugs automatically when code changes, before users see them
Tests are optional and not really needed

questions.explanation: Tests verify your code works correctly every time you make a change. Without tests, you're manually checking everything — and humans miss things.

questions.text: Your page loads 100 users, then makes a separate database query for each user's profile picture. What's this called?
questions.context: A database query is a request to get data. Making too many queries makes your app slow.
questions.explanation: The N+1 problem: 1 query to get the list + N queries for each item's details. The fix: use a JOIN or batch query to get everything in 1-2 queries.

questions.text: Your 500-line file handles database queries, API routing, AND HTML rendering. What principle does this violate?
questions.context: Code organization is about keeping your codebase clean and maintainable.
questions.explanation: Separation of concerns means each file/module should do ONE thing. Database logic, API handling, and rendering should be in separate files.

questions.text: AI generated a function that uses a library method you've never seen. What should you do?
questions.context: AI coding tools sometimes generate code that looks correct but uses APIs or methods that don't actually exist.

options.text: Trust it — AI knows all the libraries
Check the library's official documentation to verify the method exists
Run it and hope for the best
Ask a different AI to verify

questions.explanation: AI 'hallucinates' — it can generate realistic-looking code that uses APIs or methods that don't exist. Always verify unfamiliar code against official docs.

questions.text: What does 'git pull origin main' do?
questions.context: A remote is a copy of your code stored on the internet (like GitHub). 'origin' is the default name for your remote.

options.text: Downloads and merges the latest changes from the remote main branch
Creates a new branch called main

questions.explanation: git pull = git fetch + git merge. It downloads the latest changes from the remote (origin) main branch and merges them into your local branch.

questions.text: You see: 'TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading "name")'. What does this mean?
questions.context: TypeError means you're trying to do something with a value that doesn't support it. 'undefined' means a variable has no value.

options.text: You're trying to access .name on something that is undefined
Your database is missing a name column

questions.explanation: This means you have something like `user.name` where `user` is `undefined`. Check where `user` is supposed to come from — it might not be loaded yet, or a function returned nothing.

questions.text: A user types '<script>alert(1)</script>' into your comment box and other users see an alert popup. What vulnerability is this?
questions.context: When user input is displayed without cleaning it, attackers can inject code that runs in other users' browsers.
questions.explanation: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) happens when an attacker injects JavaScript that runs in other users' browsers. The fix: sanitize all user input before displaying it.

questions.text: Which prompt will give you BETTER results from an AI coding tool?

options.text: Create a Next.js login page with email/password fields, form validation, Supabase auth, error messages, and redirect to /dashboard on success

questions.explanation: Specific, detailed prompts get better results. Include the framework, features, error handling, and expected behavior. The more context AI has, the better its output.

questions.context: CI = Continuous Integration. CD = Continuous Deployment. A pipeline is a series of automated steps.

options.text: A physical pipe that connects servers
Automated processes that test and deploy your code when you push changes
A way to send emails to your team

questions.explanation: CI/CD automates the boring parts: when you push code, it automatically runs tests (CI) and deploys to production if tests pass (CD). No manual steps needed.
